# Module 3: Basic Assembly on the Cut Page

## The Anatomy of a Cut

Imagine a woodworker building a table. Before they can sand the wood, stain it, and apply a protective finish, they must first saw the rough lumber to size and tack the pieces together. In video editing, this initial phase is called the **rough cut** (or assembly). It is the stage where you stop looking at a bin of raw clips and start looking at a sequence. You aren't worrying about perfect timing, smooth transitions, or color grading yet. You are simply deciding which clips make the cut, where they begin, and what order they go in. 

To do this, we will rely primarily on the **Cut Page**, which was designed specifically for fast, high-speed assembly. 

Before we start placing clips, we need to understand the canvas we will be painting on. In DaVinci Resolve, your video is built on two fundamental structures: the timeline and the track.

### The Timeline and Tracks

The **timeline** is the horizontal area at the bottom half of the Cut Page. It is a visual representation of your video over time. If you look at the timeline from left to right, you are looking at the beginning of your video to the end. 

Within the timeline, you will see horizontal lanes. These lanes are called **tracks**. Think of tracks as layers of transparent film stacked on top of one another. 
*   **Video Tracks** display images. Whatever is on a higher video track (like Video Track 2) will visually cover up whatever is directly beneath it on a lower video track (like Video Track 1). 
*   **Audio Tracks** hold sound. Unlike video, audio tracks don't cover each other up; they mix together. If you have music on Audio Track 1 and dialogue on Audio Track 2, the viewer will hear both simultaneously.

When you place a clip on the timeline, you are placing a specific segment of video and its accompanying audio onto these tracks. 

## Setting Up Your Workspace

If you have just completed the media import process from the previous chapter, your screen should be populated with the clips you brought in. To make sure we are on the same page, click the **Cut Page** icon at the very bottom of the DaVinci Resolve interface (it looks like a pair of scissors). 

On the Cut Page, the interface is divided into a few key areas. For this chapter, we are concerned with three of them:
1.  **The Media Pool (Top Left):** This is where all your imported clips live. 
2.  **The Source Viewer (Top Right):** This is a preview screen where you can watch a clip *before* you decide to put it on the timeline. 
3.  **The Timeline (Bottom Half):** This is where your rough cut will live. 

If you don't have a timeline open yet, you can quickly create one by right-clicking in the empty gray space of the Media Pool and selecting **Create New Timeline**. A window will pop up asking for settings, but for now, you can leave the defaults (which should match your **Project Settings** for **Timeline Resolution** and **Timeline Frame Rate**) and click **Create**. You will now see an empty timeline at the bottom of your screen.

## Using the Source Viewer

The biggest mistake beginner editors make is dragging every single clip they imported straight onto the timeline. If you shot an hour of footage for a three-minute video, doing this will leave you with a chaotic, unmanageable timeline. 

Instead, we use the **Source Viewer**. The Source Viewer allows you to screen your raw footage, find the exact moment the action begins, find the exact moment the action ends, and send *only* that specific portion to the timeline. 

To do this, we use **Marks**. Specifically, we use **In points** and **Out points**. 
*   An **In point** marks where you want a clip to begin.
*   An **Out point** marks where you want a clip to end.

Let’s walk through a realistic scenario. Suppose you are editing a short promotional video for a local coffee shop. You have a clip in your Media Pool called "Barista_Pour_Latte.mov". The clip is 30 seconds long, but it features the barista setting up the cup, pouring the latte, and then walking away to wipe down the counter. You only want the actual pour.

### Marking In and Out Points

1.  Double-click "Barista_Pour_Latte.mov" in the Media Pool. It will load into the Source Viewer on the top right, and you will see the video appear.
2.  Press the Spacebar to play the clip. Watch the barista set up the cup. 
3.  When the barista lifts the milk pitcher and begins to pour, press the Spacebar to pause the video. 
4.  To mark this exact frame as your starting point, press the **I** key on your keyboard (I for In). You will see a small white bracket appear at the bottom of the viewer, and a gray shaded region will begin to form on the timeline scrubber.
5.  Press the Spacebar to resume playback. Watch the latte pour into the cup. 
6.  Just as the pour finishes and the barista starts to pull the pitcher away, press the Spacebar to pause.
7.  To mark this as your ending point, press the **O** key on your keyboard (O for Out). The bracket will appear here, and the gray region between the In and Out points is now highlighted. 

You have just created a **marked clip**. You have isolated a 5-second segment from a 30-second file. 

*Note: If you make a mistake, you can press **Option+X** (Mac) or **Alt+X** (Windows) to clear your In and Out points and start over.*

## Performing Your First Edits

Now that you have a segment marked, it is time to place it on the timeline. DaVinci Resolve offers several ways to do this, but we will focus on the two most fundamental methods for building a rough cut quickly: the Append edit and the Overwrite edit.

### The Append Edit

The **Append edit** is the simplest way to build a sequence. When you append a clip, Resolve places it at the very end of your timeline. If the timeline is empty, it goes to the beginning. If you already have clips on the timeline, it snaps directly onto the tail end of the last clip.

Let's append our marked latte pour to the timeline:

1.  Ensure your In and Out points are still marked on the "Barista_Pour_Latte.mov" clip in the Source Viewer.
2.  Look at the toolbar directly above the timeline. You will see an icon that looks like a rectangle with an arrow pointing to the right, entering from the back. Hover your mouse over it, and a tooltip will say "Append".
3.  Click the **Append** button. 

Alternatively, you can simply press the **F9** key on your keyboard (or **Shift+F9** depending on your keyboard layout, though clicking the button is foolproof for beginners). 

Look down at your timeline. The 5-second latte pour has been placed on Video Track 1 and Audio Track 1. A thin vertical line, called the **playhead**, will be resting right at the end of the clip you just added. 

**A scenario for building a sequence:**
You want to build a montage of the coffee shop. You mark In and Out points on a clip of "Roasting_Beans.mov" and click Append. It snaps to the end of the latte pour. You mark In and Out points on "Customer_Smiling.mov" and click Append. It snaps to the end of the roasting beans clip. By repeating this process, you are rapidly stringing together a sequence of events in chronological order.

### The Overwrite Edit

The **Overwrite edit** is slightly different. Instead of appending to the end of the timeline, an overwrite edit places your marked clip exactly where your playhead is currently resting. If there are already clips on the timeline where you are placing the new clip, the new clip will "overwrite" or replace whatever was there before. It does not push other clips down the timeline; it simply pastes over them.

Why would you use this? Imagine you have appended five clips together, but you realize the third clip—the latte pour—is slightly too dark. You found a better take called "Barista_Pour_Latte_Take2.mov". You don't want to disrupt the order of your sequence; you just want to swap out the bad clip for the good one.

1.  Move your playhead by clicking anywhere over the latte pour clip currently sitting on your timeline. The playhead will snap to that location.
2.  Double-click "Barista_Pour_Latte_Take2.mov" in your Media Pool to load it into the Source Viewer.
3.  Mark your new In and Out points for the better take.
4.  Look at the toolbar above the timeline. Find the icon that looks like a rectangle with an arrow pointing straight down. Hover over it, and the tooltip will say "Overwrite".
5.  Click the **Overwrite** button (or press **F10**).

The new take replaces the old one. If your new marked segment is exactly the same length as the old one, the timeline remains perfectly intact. If the new segment is longer, it will overwrite part of the next clip. If it is shorter, it will leave a gap. 

## Rearranging Clips in the Timeline

A rough cut is called "rough" for a reason. As you watch your sequence back, you will inevitably realize that a clip belongs somewhere else. Maybe the shot of the customer smiling works better *before* the shot of the beans roasting, rather than after.

DaVinci Resolve makes rearranging clips incredibly intuitive on the Cut Page. 

### Moving Clips
To move a clip, simply hover your mouse over the clip block in the timeline, click and hold the left mouse button, and drag the clip to a new location. As you drag, you will see a thin white vertical line appear between other clips. This line indicates where the clip will be dropped. Release the mouse button, and the clip snaps into its new home. 

When you drag a clip into the middle of an existing sequence, Resolve automatically pushes the other clips down the timeline to make room for it. 

### Swapping Clips
Sometimes you don't want to push clips down; you want to swap two clips without altering the overall length of your sequence. To do this, hold down the **Option** key (Mac) or **Alt** key (Windows) while clicking and dragging a clip over another clip. Release the mouse button, and the two clips will swap places. 

## Deleting Clips

Removing unwanted clips is just as important as placing them. There are two primary ways to delete a clip in DaVinci Resolve, and they behave very differently. 

### The Standard Delete (Ripple Delete)
When you want to completely remove a clip and close the gap it leaves behind, you perform a **Ripple Delete**. This is the most common deletion method during the rough cut phase.

1.  Click once on the clip you want to remove. It will be outlined in red to show it is selected.
2.  Press the **Backspace** key (or **Delete** on some keyboards), OR right-click the clip and select **Ripple Delete** from the menu.

The clip disappears, and all the clips to the right of it slide to the left to fill the empty space. Your timeline remains continuous. 

### The Lift Delete
Sometimes you want to remove a clip, but you *want* to keep the empty space. This is common if you are trying to sync something up to a specific time, or if you plan to fill that gap with a different clip later. This is called a **Lift**.

1.  Click once on the clip you want to remove.
2.  Press **Shift+Delete** (or **Shift+Backspace**).

The clip disappears, but it leaves a blank, empty gap on the timeline. The clips to the right do not move. 

## A Practical Assembly Workflow

Let’s tie these tools together into a realistic workflow for building a rough cut. 

You are editing a 60-second highlight video of a weekend hiking trip. You have imported 20 clips into your Media Pool. 

1.  **Screen and Mark:** Double-click your first clip, "Trailhead_Start.mov". Watch it in the Source Viewer. Mark an In point when your friend’s foot first steps onto the trail. Mark an Out point when they pass the camera. 
2.  **Place:** Press **F9** (or click Append) to place this on the timeline. 
3.  **Repeat:** Double-click "Forest_Walk.mov". Mark the In and Out points for the best 4 seconds of footage. Press **F9**. 
4.  **Continue Building:** Repeat this process for "Stream_Crossing.mov", "Summit_View.mov", and "Pack_Lunch.mov". You now have a 25-second sequence on your timeline.
5.  **Review:** Press the Spacebar to play the timeline from the beginning. 
6.  **Rearrange:** You realize the "Summit_View" clip would make a much stronger opening. Click and drag the "Summit_View" clip from the middle of the timeline and drop it at the very beginning, before the "Trailhead_Start" clip. 
7.  **Swap:** You decide the "Forest_Walk" clip and the "Stream_Crossing" clip are in the wrong order. Hold **Option/Alt**, drag "Forest_Walk" over "Stream_Crossing", and release to swap them.
8.  **Delete:** The "Pack_Lunch" clip feels too slow. Click it and press **Backspace** to ripple delete it. The timeline closes the gap automatically.
9.  **Overwrite:** You find a better angle of the stream crossing. Move your playhead over the current "Stream_Crossing" clip. Load the new angle in the Source Viewer, mark your points, and press **F10** to overwrite the old clip.

In less than ten minutes, you have screened your footage, selected the best moments, arranged them in a logical order, and refined the sequence. You have built a rough cut. 

## Key Takeaways
*   A **timeline** is the horizontal workspace where you assemble your video over time, made up of horizontal lanes called **tracks**.
*   **Video tracks** layer on top of one another (higher tracks cover lower tracks), while **audio tracks** mix together.
*   The **Source Viewer** allows you to preview raw footage and select specific segments before committing them to the timeline.
*   **In points (I)** and **Out points (O)** define the beginning and end of the segment you want to use from a source clip.
*   An **Append edit (F9)** places a marked clip at the very end of your timeline, making it ideal for building a sequence chronologically.
*   An **Overwrite edit (F10)** places a marked clip at the playhead, replacing any existing clips at that location without pushing other clips down the timeline.
*   You can **rearrange clips** by clicking and dragging them; holding **Option/Alt** while dragging will swap two clips without altering the timeline length.
*   A **Ripple Delete (Backspace)** removes a clip and closes the gap, while a **Lift (Shift+Delete)** removes a clip but leaves an empty gap behind.

# Module 4: Refining Edits on the Edit Page

## The Difference Between a Rough Cut and a Final Edit

Imagine you are watching a conversation between two characters in a movie. The camera cuts to Character A, who finishes speaking. Then, there is a long, awkward pause before the camera cuts to Character B, who finally replies. It feels unnatural, dragging the energy of the scene down. In a professional film, that pause would be trimmed down to a fraction of a second, making the dialogue feel snappy and real. 

In Chapter 3, we used the **Cut Page** to quickly assemble our clips, dragging them onto the timeline to build a basic story. That process gives you what editors call a "rough cut"—a sequence of clips placed in the right order. But a rough cut rarely has perfect timing. Clips might run a few frames too long, a cut might feel jarring, or a scene might transition too abruptly. 

To fix these issues, we need to move from the **Cut Page** to the **Edit Page**. While the Cut Page is designed for speed and throwing a story together, the Edit Page is designed for precision. It is the traditional, frame-by-frame workspace where editors polish the timing and flow of a video. 

## Getting Oriented on the Edit Page

Before we start making adjustments, let’s look at where we are working. At the bottom of the DaVinci Resolve interface, click the **Edit Page** icon (it looks like a timeline with a playhead). 

If you completed the assembly from Chapter 3, your timeline will look very similar here, but the layout offers more room for detail. You will notice the timeline takes up a large portion of the bottom half of the screen, while the viewer occupies the top. The Edit Page gives you access to advanced tools that allow you to manipulate the exact points where two clips touch—known as the **edit point**. 

Every edit point has two sides: the outgoing clip (the **A-side**) and the incoming clip (the **B-side**). When we talk about trimming, we are talking about adjusting exactly where these two clips meet.

## The Blade Tool: Splitting Clips Precisely

Sometimes, you don't need to adjust the boundary between two clips; you need to split a single clip into two pieces. Maybe you want to remove a stumble in the middle of an interview, or perhaps you want to insert a cutaway shot right in the middle of a continuous video clip. 

To do this, you use the **Blade Tool**.

1. Locate the toolbar directly above your timeline. You will see an icon that looks like a straight razor blade.
2. Click the Blade Tool to activate it. Your cursor will change into a small blade icon.
3. Move your cursor over the clip you want to split on the timeline.
4. Click exactly where you want the cut to happen. 

The clip is now split into two independent clips. You can select the standard arrow tool (the **Pointer Tool**) again, click on the newly created clip, and move or delete it without affecting the other half. 

**A faster way to blade:** You do not actually need to click the Blade Tool icon every time. A common professional shortcut is to simply hover your cursor over the timeline where you want to cut and press the **B** key on your keyboard. The cursor temporarily becomes the Blade Tool, you click to make your cut, and when you move the cursor away, it reverts to the Pointer Tool. Pressing **A** on your keyboard also instantly switches you back to the standard Pointer Tool.

## Advanced Trimming Tools

When you assembled your rough cut on the Cut Page, you likely dragged clips from your media pool and dropped them next to each other. But what if a clip plays for a second too long before the next clip starts? 

You could click and drag the edge of the clip inward to shorten it, but doing this with a mouse can be imprecise. You might accidentally leave a gap between clips, or you might misalign your video and audio. To solve this, DaVinci Resolve has a dedicated set of **Trim Tools**. 

Let’s look at the four main trim tools, what they do, and when to use them.

### The Ripple Tool

The **Ripple Tool** adjusts the duration of a single clip without leaving a gap. When you shorten a clip with the Ripple Tool, all the clips to the right of it slide over to fill the empty space, and your overall timeline gets shorter. 

**When to use it:** Use the Ripple Tool when a clip is too long, but you want the rest of your video to play immediately after it without any dead space.

**How to use it:**
1. Select the Ripple Tool from the toolbar (or press the **R** key).
2. Hover over the edge of the clip you want to adjust.
3. Click and drag the edge inward (to shorten) or outward (to lengthen, if the original media has extra footage available).
4. Notice that as you drag, the adjacent clips move with you. The total length of your timeline changes.

*Scenario:* You have a five-second clip of a car driving by, but the last two seconds are just an empty street. Using the Ripple Tool, grab the right edge of the clip and drag it to the left by two seconds. The next clip in your timeline immediately shifts left, closing the gap. Your timeline is now two seconds shorter.

### The Roll Tool

The **Roll Tool** adjusts the boundary between two adjacent clips. Instead of changing the total length of your timeline, it takes time from one clip and gives it to the other. If you shorten Clip A by one second, Clip B automatically gets one second longer. 

**When to use it:** Use the Roll Tool when your overall timeline duration is perfect, but the exact moment a cut happens feels a little too early or too late. 

**How to use it:**
1. Select the Roll Tool from the toolbar (or press the **R** key twice to cycle from Ripple to Roll).
2. Hover directly over the edit point between two clips.
3. Click and drag left or right. 
4. Watch as the cut point moves. The outgoing clip gets shorter while the incoming clip gets longer (or vice versa), but the rest of your timeline doesn't move at all.

*Scenario:* You have a clip of a person raising a glass, followed by a clip of their friend cheering. The cut to the friend happens right as the glass reaches the person's lips, but it feels too fast. Using the Roll Tool, drag the edit point slightly to the right. Now you see the person hold the glass at their lips for a moment longer before cutting to the friend. The total length of the video hasn't changed, but the flow feels more natural.

### The Slip Tool

The **Slip Tool** changes what portion of a clip you see, without changing its duration or its position on the timeline. Imagine you have a 10-second clip, but you only placed 3 seconds of it on your timeline. The Slip Tool lets you choose *which* 3 seconds are visible.

**When to use it:** Use the Slip Tool when a clip is the right length and is in the right place in your sequence, but the action happening inside the clip is slightly off. 

**How to use it:**
1. Select the Slip Tool from the toolbar (or press the **S** key).
2. Click on the body of the clip you want to adjust.
3. Drag left or right. 
4. The clip stays exactly where it is on the timeline, but the video playing inside it shifts. DaVinci Resolve will often show you a split-screen viewer: one side showing the first frame of your clip, and the other showing the last frame, so you can see exactly where your new selection begins and ends.

*Scenario:* You have a three-second clip of a dog turning its head. Right now, the clip starts with the dog looking away, and ends just before it looks at the camera. You want the clip to end with the dog looking directly at the lens. Using the Slip Tool, click and drag the clip slightly to the left. The clip remains three seconds long and stays in the exact same spot in your video, but now the dog turns its head and ends up looking at the camera.

### The Slide Tool

The **Slide Tool** moves a clip left or right along the timeline, effectively changing its position relative to the clips around it. To make room for this, the Slide Tool automatically adjusts the durations of the clips immediately next to it. 

**When to use it:** Use the Slide Tool when a clip is the right length, but it needs to happen a little earlier or a little later in your sequence, and you don't want to create a gap or overlap your other clips.

**How to use it:**
1. Select the Slide Tool from the toolbar.
2. Click on the body of the clip you want to move.
3. Drag left or right. 
4. The clip moves, but the outgoing clip (to the left) and the incoming clip (to the right) will shrink or grow to accommodate the move. The total length of your timeline remains unchanged.

*Scenario:* You have a sequence: Clip A (a man walking), Clip B (a close-up of his shoes), and Clip C (him arriving at a door). You want Clip B to happen a little later. If you use the Slide Tool on Clip B and drag it to the right, Clip A will automatically get longer, and Clip C will automatically get shorter to make room. Clip B slides later in the sequence without messing up your overall timeline duration.

## Adding and Customizing Video Transitions

Hard cuts—where one clip instantly becomes the next—are the most common type of edit. But sometimes, you want a softer shift between scenes. This is where **transitions** come in. 

The most classic video transition is the **cross dissolve**. In a cross dissolve, the first clip gradually fades out while the second clip gradually fades in, with the two images blending together in the middle.

### How to Add a Transition

1. Look at the top-left corner of the Edit Page interface. You will see a panel called **Effects**. (If you don't see it, go to the top menu bar, click **Workspace**, and select **Effects**).
2. Click on **Video Transitions** to expand the folder.
3. You will see a list of transitions, including **Cross Dissolve**, **Dip to Color Dissolve**, **Wipe**, and others.
4. To add a transition, simply click and drag the transition name from the Effects panel directly onto the edit point between two clips on your timeline.
5. Drop it directly on the cut. 

When you drop a transition, you will see a small rectangular box appear over the edit point, representing the transition. 

### Customizing Transition Duration

By default, DaVinci Resolve applies a cross dissolve that lasts for a specific number of frames. But you will almost always want to change how long the transition takes. A fast dissolve might take 5 frames (a fraction of a second), while a slow, dramatic dissolve might take 2 seconds.

**Method 1: Dragging the edge**
Hover your cursor over the edge of the transition box on your timeline. Your cursor will change into a trim bracket. Click and drag the edge outward to make the transition longer, or inward to make it shorter. 

**Method 2: Typing the exact duration**
For precise control, you can type exactly how long you want the transition to be.
1. Click on the transition box to select it (it will outline in red).
2. Look at the top-right corner of the timeline area. You will see a small text box displaying the current duration (e.g., `00:00:00:10`, which means 10 frames).
3. Click that text box, type your desired duration (for example, type `15` for 15 frames, or `100` for 1 second), and press Enter. 

*Note on timecodes:* DaVinci Resolve measures time using the standard timecode format: Hours:Minutes:Seconds:Frames. If you type a simple number like `20`, Resolve assumes you mean 20 frames. If you want a transition that lasts exactly one second, and your **Timeline Frame Rate** is 24 frames per second, you can type `24` or `1:00`.

### Transition Alignment

When you drop a transition on a cut, you can control how it aligns. 
*   **Centered:** The transition overlaps equally on both clips (half on the outgoing clip, half on the incoming clip). 
*   **Start on Cut:** The transition begins exactly at the cut, meaning the outgoing clip stays solid until the very end, and the incoming clip fades in.
*   **End on Cut:** The transition ends exactly at the cut, meaning the outgoing clip fades out, and the incoming clip starts fully solid.

You can change the alignment by right-clicking the transition box on the timeline, hovering over **Transition Alignment**, and selecting your preferred option. Just be aware that to use "Start on Cut" or "End on Cut," the clips usually need to have extra, unused media (called **handles**) at their edges. If a clip ends exactly at the cut with no extra footage, Resolve cannot fade it out gradually.

## Utilizing the Inspector: Speed and Scale

Sometimes, fixing the flow of a video isn't about where you cut, but how the clip itself plays. You might need to speed up a slow walk down a hallway, or zoom in on a clip to reframe a subject. For these adjustments, we use the **Inspector**.

The Inspector is a panel usually located in the top-right corner of the Edit Page. If you don't see it, click the **Inspector** button in the top-right corner of the interface to toggle it open. The Inspector is context-sensitive: whatever clip you have selected on your timeline, the Inspector will show you the controls to modify that specific clip.

### Changing Clip Speed

Changing the speed of a clip alters how fast or slow the action plays. 

1. Select a clip on your timeline.
2. Open the Inspector and click the **Video** tab.
3. Scroll down until you find the **Speed** section.
4. Check the box next to **Speed Change** to enable it.
5. You will see a percentage value (100% is normal speed). 
    * To make the clip play twice as fast, change the percentage to **200%**. 
    * To make it play in slow motion (half speed), change the percentage to **50%**.

When you change the speed in the Inspector, DaVinci Resolve automatically adjusts the clip's duration on your timeline. A clip sped up to 200% will take up half the space it used to. 

*Tip:* If you want to change a clip's speed without pushing all the subsequent clips out of place, hold down the **Option** key (on Mac) or the **Alt** key (on Windows) while dragging the edge of the clip with the Ripple Tool. This is called a "ripple speed change" and is a quick way to fit a clip into a specific gap by speeding it up or slowing it down.

### Changing Clip Scale (Zooming In)

**Scale** refers to the size of the video image. By default, a clip is placed on the timeline at 100% scale, meaning it fits the **Timeline Resolution** exactly as it was shot. However, if you shot your video in 4K but your timeline is set to 1080p HD, you have a lot of extra resolution to work with. You can scale the clip up to 200% or more to zoom in without losing quality.

Even if your clip and timeline are the same resolution, you can scale a clip up slightly (e.g., to 110% or 120%) to reframe a shot—perhaps to cut off a microphone that dipped into the top of the frame, or to make a subject appear closer to the camera.

1. Select a clip on your timeline.
2. Open the Inspector and ensure you are on the **Video** tab.
3. Look for the **Zoom** or **Scale** setting. 
4. Click and drag the number to the right to increase the scale, or type a specific number (like `115`).
5. When you scale a clip up, the image will zoom in on the center. To move the focal point, look slightly below the Scale setting for the **Position** parameters. Adjusting the X (horizontal) and Y (vertical) position numbers will pan the zoomed-in image left, right, up, or down until it is framed exactly how you want it.

## Key Takeaways

*   The **Edit Page** is the precision workspace for polishing timing, whereas the Cut Page is for fast assembly.
*   The **Blade Tool** (shortcut: B) splits a single clip into two independent pieces.
*   **Ripple Tool** shortens or lengthens a clip, sliding all subsequent clips over to close the gap, changing the total timeline duration.
*   **Roll Tool** moves the cut point between two adjacent clips, making one shorter and the other longer without changing the overall timeline duration.
*   **Slip Tool** changes which portion of a clip's media is visible, without moving the clip's position or changing its duration on the timeline.
*   **Slide Tool** moves a clip left or right in the sequence, automatically adjusting the duration of the adjacent clips to make room.
*   **Transitions** (like the Cross Dissolve) are found in the Effects panel and can be dragged onto an edit point. Their duration can be customized by dragging the transition edges or typing a specific frame count.
*   The **Inspector** allows you to modify individual clips, including changing their playback **Speed** (percentage) and **Scale** (zooming in to reframe the shot).

# Module 5: Text, Titles, and Basic Effects

Imagine watching a documentary where a subject is introduced, but their name never appears on screen. Or a tutorial video where the instructor references a specific website, but you have to frantically scribble down the URL before the scene changes. Text and basic visual effects are the unsung heroes of video editing. They provide context, guide the viewer’s attention, and add a layer of professional polish that separates a rough assembly of clips from a finished product.

Now that you have assembled and refined your clips on the Edit Page, it is time to elevate your timeline. We will move beyond simple cuts and trims to add textual elements, apply basic visual effects, and introduce movement to static shots. 

## The Titles Library: Adding Text to Your Timeline

In DaVinci Resolve, adding text is not a single, rigid tool. Instead, Resolve provides a dedicated library of pre-built text templates. You can access these by clicking the **Effects** button in the upper-left corner of the Edit Page (or by pressing `Shift+5`), which opens the Effects Library. 

On the left side of this panel, you will see a category called **Titles**. If you expand this, you will find a variety of options. 

### Text vs. Text+ vs. Fusion Title
When you browse the Titles folder, you will notice three main types of text:

*   **Text:** This is the most basic, lightweight text option. It renders incredibly fast and is perfect for simple lower thirds, titles, or closing credits. If you just need words on the screen, start here.
*   **Text+:** This is a more advanced text generator powered by Resolve’s Fusion technology (which we will briefly touch on later). It allows for character-level animation, custom paths, and complex styling. It is more resource-intensive than the basic Text tool.
*   **Fusion Titles:** These are pre-designed, highly customizable title templates. They often include built-in animations, backgrounds, and decorative elements. They are essentially mini-compositions built on the Fusion Page.

### Placing and Formatting a Title
To add a title to your video, simply click and drag your chosen title from the Effects Library down into your timeline. When you drag a title over a video clip, you have two placement options:

1.  **Overwrite (Drop on top of a clip):** If you drop the title directly onto an existing video clip, it will overwrite that portion of the video with the title. 
2.  **Insert (Drop between clips):** If you drop the title in the gap between two clips, it will push the clips apart to make room for the title.

Once the title is on your timeline, it becomes its own clip. To change the words and styling, double-click the title clip in the timeline. This loads the title’s settings into the **Inspector**, located in the top-right corner of the Edit Page.

In the Inspector, you will find controls for:
*   **Text:** The actual words you want to display.
*   **Font:** The typeface (e.g., Arial, Helvetica, Courier).
*   **Size and Position:** How large the text is and where it sits on the screen. You can drag the text directly in the viewer, or type specific X (horizontal) and Y (vertical) coordinates in the Inspector.
*   **Color:** A color picker to change the text fill, outline, or drop shadow.

### Scenario: Creating a Lower Third
A **lower third** is a graphic placed in the lower area of the screen, usually to identify a person or a location. Let’s create one.

1. Drag the basic **Text** title from the Effects Library onto your timeline, placing it above a clip of a person talking. Ensure it does not completely cover their face.
2. Double-click the new Text clip to open the Inspector.
3. In the Text field, type the person's name (e.g., "Jane Doe").
4. Change the Font to something clean, like Arial or Open Sans.
5. Adjust the Size to roughly 40.
6. In the Viewer window, click and drag the text down to the lower third of the screen.
7. To add a professional touch, scroll down in the Inspector to the **Background** section. Check the box to enable a background, set the color to black, and lower the opacity to 50%. This creates a semi-transparent banner that makes the text readable against the video.

## Introduction to OpenFX

Text is only the beginning. Sometimes you need to alter the look of the video itself. For this, DaVinci Resolve uses **OpenFX**. 

OpenFX is an open standard for visual effects plug-ins. In simpler terms, it is a universal format that allows third-party developers to create effects that work inside various video editing programs. DaVinci Resolve comes with a robust library of built-in OpenFX plug-ins.

To access them, open the Effects Library (`Shift+5`) and click on **OpenFX** in the left-hand column. Here you will find categories like Blur, Resolve FX Color, Resolve FX Light, and Stylize.

### Applying and Modifying Gaussian Blur
One of the most commonly used effects is the **Gaussian Blur**. A Gaussian Blur is a specific type of blur that softens an image by averaging the colors of neighboring pixels, resulting in a smooth, natural-looking out-of-focus effect.

**Scenario:** You are editing an interview, and the interviewee mentions a sensitive topic. You want to obscure their face momentarily to protect their identity.

1. In the OpenFX library, locate **Gaussian Blur** (you can use the search bar at the top of the Effects Library).
2. Click and drag the Gaussian Blur effect onto the interview clip in your timeline.
3. Once applied, a small gray badge with an "fx" icon will appear on the clip, indicating an effect is active.
4. Double-click the clip to open the Inspector, then click the **Effects** tab at the top of the Inspector.
5. You will see the Gaussian Blur controls. Increase the **Horizontal Radius** and **Vertical Radius** sliders until the face is sufficiently obscured. 

By default, the blur will affect the entire clip for its entire duration. Later in this chapter, we will look at how to isolate effects to specific parts of the screen, and how to turn them on and off over time.

## Keyframing: Animating Properties Over Time

Up to this point, any change you have made—like cropping a clip, changing a title's size, or applying a blur—has been static. If you set a blur radius to 50, it stays at 50 from the first frame to the last. 

To make a video dynamic, properties need to change over time. A title might start small and zoom in, or a blur might slowly fade away. This is accomplished through **keyframing**.

A **keyframe** is a marker that records the specific value of a property (like size, position, or opacity) at a specific point in time. By placing at least two keyframes on a clip—with different values—the software automatically calculates the transition between them. This automatic calculation is called **interpolation**.

### How to Animate a Title
Let’s use keyframes to make our lower third title slide onto the screen from the left, rather than just appearing instantly.

1. Select your Text clip in the timeline and open the Inspector.
2. Move the playhead to the very beginning of the Text clip. (You can use the arrow keys to step frame-by-frame).
3. In the Inspector, find the **Position** controls (under the Layout tab). 
4. Change the X (horizontal) coordinate to a negative number (e.g., -1.0 or lower) until the text is completely off the left edge of the Viewer.
5. To the right of the Position setting, you will see a small diamond icon. This is the **Keyframe button**. Click it. A small gray diamond will appear on the clip in your timeline, indicating a keyframe has been set.
6. Move the playhead forward in the timeline—perhaps one second into the clip.
7. Change the X coordinate back to a normal value so the text sits comfortably in the lower third of the screen.
8. Because you changed a property while the playhead is at a new time, Resolve automatically creates a second keyframe.

Play back the clip. The text will now smoothly slide in from the left edge of the screen. If you ever need to adjust the timing of the animation, you can open the clip by double-clicking it, clicking the small arrow next to the Keyframe button in the Inspector, and selecting **Keyframe Editor**. This opens a visual graph where you can drag your keyframes left or right to speed up or slow down the animation.

## Compositing: Basic Picture-in-Picture

**Compositing** is the process of combining multiple visual elements from separate sources into single images. It is how you create the illusion that all elements were part of the same scene. The most common compositing technique in basic video editing is the **Picture-in-Picture** (PiP).

A Picture-in-Picture effect involves overlaying a smaller video clip on top of a larger, full-screen background video. You see this frequently in gaming streams, reaction videos, and software tutorials.

### Creating a Picture-in-Picture Effect
DaVinci Resolve makes PiP effects incredibly straightforward using its built-in compositing architecture.

1. Ensure you have two video clips on your timeline. Place the clip you want to be the background on **Video Track 1** (V1). 
2. Place the clip you want to be the smaller, foreground video on **Video Track 2** (V2), directly above the background clip. 

*Note: In DaVinci Resolve, video tracks are layered from bottom to top. V2 will always appear in front of V1.*

3. Select the clip on V2. 
4. Open the Inspector and navigate to the **Transform** settings.
5. To shrink the clip, lower the **Zoom** value (e.g., to 0.35 or 35%). 
6. Adjust the **Position** values (X and Y) to move the smaller video to a corner of the screen, such as the bottom right.
7. To make the PiP look more professional, you can add a border or a drop shadow. In the Inspector, scroll down to the **Border** section, enable it, set a solid color (like white), and give it a small width (e.g., 0.02). You can also enable a **Drop Shadow** to lift the PiP visually off the background.

### Animating the Picture-in-Picture
Because you now know how to keyframe, you can combine these techniques. You can animate the PiP to scale up from nothing when the speaker starts talking, or slide off-screen when they finish. Simply set a keyframe for the Zoom or Position at the start of the clip, move the playhead, and change the value.

## Advanced Compositing: The Alpha Output and Masks

Sometimes, you need to affect only a specific portion of a clip. For instance, returning to our earlier scenario: you want to blur a face, but only while the interviewee is speaking, and you do not want to blur the rest of the background. 

To do this, you need to isolate the effect. While Resolve has advanced tools for this on the Color Page, you can achieve it right on the Edit Page using a combination of a duplicate clip, a mask, and an Alpha Output.

### Setting Up an Alpha Output
An **Alpha Output** is a special track in your timeline that tells DaVinci Resolve to treat the combined transparency of your video tracks as a single output. It is required if you want to "cut a hole" in a video clip using a mask.

1. Right-click in the empty, dark gray space of your timeline track header area (where the track lock and mute buttons are).
2. Select **Add Alpha Output**. A green track labeled "Alpha 1" will appear at the bottom of your timeline.

### Masking a Specific Area
To blur just a face, you need to separate the face from the background.

1. Select the interview clip on V1. Hold `Alt` (Windows) or `Option` (Mac) and drag the clip straight up to **Video Track 2** (V2). This duplicates the clip exactly above the original. You now have two identical clips.
2. Turn off the visibility (the small eye icon) on the V2 track temporarily so you can see the original clip on V1.
3. Select the V1 clip. Open the Inspector, go to the **Effects** tab, and apply the **Gaussian Blur** effect. Crank the blur radius up until the entire frame is heavily blurred. 
4. Turn the visibility of V2 back on. The sharp video is now completely covering the blurred video. We need to cut a hole in the V2 clip so the blurred V1 clip shows through, but only over the face.

### Drawing the Mask and Connecting the Nodes
DaVinci Resolve handles this masking through a node-based workflow directly on the Edit Page.

1. Double-click the V2 clip to open it in the **Viewer**. 
2. At the bottom left of the Viewer, click the dropdown menu that says **Clip** and change it to **Fusion**. (Alternatively, click the small "circle and square" icon to open the Inspector's Fusion tab).
3. You will see a visual flowchart with two boxes (nodes): **MediaIn1** and **MediaOut1**.
4. Click the small square icon in the Inspector to add a **Rectangle Mask** (or use the Ellipse mask for a rounder face). A new node called **Rectangle1** will appear.
5. Click and drag from the small yellow square on the right side of the **Rectangle1** node, and drop it onto the small yellow square on the left side of the **MediaIn1** node. This connects the mask to the video.
6. In the Viewer, you will now see a rectangle. Drag the corners of this rectangle to cover the person's face. Because the mask is applied to V2, it is cutting a hole in the sharp V2 clip, revealing the blurred V1 clip underneath.

### Tracking the Mask
A person's face rarely stays perfectly still. DaVinci Resolve includes a powerful **Tracker** that can automatically follow the movement of the face.

1. In the Inspector, with the Rectangle mask still selected, scroll down to the **Tracking** section.
2. Click the **Play** button (or forward arrow) next to "Track Forward". Resolve will analyze the clip frame-by-frame, moving the rectangle to follow the face.
3. Once tracking is complete, play back the timeline. The blur will now stay locked to the person's face as they move, leaving the rest of the background sharp.

*Note: This brief introduction to nodes touches on the **Fusion Page**, DaVinci Resolve’s dedicated visual effects and compositing environment. While we are using it here on the Edit Page for a specific task, the Fusion Page offers vastly more power for complex effects, which you can explore further as you advance.*

## Key Takeaways

*   **Titles Library:** DaVinci Resolve provides pre-built text templates in the Effects Library. Use the basic **Text** title for simple, fast-rendering text, and **Text+** or **Fusion Titles** for advanced styling and built-in animations.
*   **Inspector:** The Inspector panel (top-right) is where you modify the properties of any selected clip, including text content, font, size, position, and applied effects.
*   **OpenFX:** This is the plug-in standard used by Resolve to apply visual effects. Effects like **Gaussian Blur** are dragged from the OpenFX library directly onto timeline clips.
*   **Keyframes:** Keyframes are markers that record a property's value at a specific point in time. By setting two or more keyframes with different values, you can animate properties (like making text slide in or a video zoom out).
*   **Compositing:** Combining multiple video layers. A **Picture-in-Picture** effect is created by placing a video on V2 above a background video on V1, and shrinking the V2 clip using the Zoom transform.
*   **Alpha Outputs and Masks:** To isolate an effect (like blurring only a face), you can duplicate a clip, blur the bottom layer, and use a mask on the top layer to cut a hole revealing the blur. Adding an Alpha Output to the timeline allows this compositing workflow to function on the Edit Page.

# Module 6: Audio Mixing and Fairlight Basics

Imagine watching an interview where the speaker's voice is muffled, a background music track is overwhelmingly loud, and a sudden door slam makes you rip your headphones off. No matter how perfect your video editing, color grading, or visual effects are, bad audio will ruin an audience's experience faster than anything else. In fact, poor sound is the most common reason viewers click away from a video.

Up to this point, you have been assembling your video primarily on the **Cut Page** and **Edit Page**, adding text, titles, and basic visual effects. While you have likely been adjusting basic audio levels along the way, DaVinci Resolve has a dedicated environment built specifically for professional audio post-production: the **Fairlight Page**. 

Named after Fairlight, a pioneering Australian audio company that Blackmagic Design acquired, this page offers studio-grade tools for mixing, effects, and sound design. But you don't need to be an audio engineer to use it. In this chapter, we will demystify the audio workflow, starting with the fundamental principles of sound in video editing, and learn how to balance levels, clean up dialogue, and automate your mix so everything sounds cohesive.

## Understanding Audio Fundamentals

Before we dive into the Fairlight interface, we need to define a few critical terms. Audio editing has its own jargon, but the underlying concepts are straightforward.

### Decibels (dB) and LUFS
Sound is measured in **Decibels (dB)**. However, in digital audio editing, we use a scale called **dBFS** (Decibels Full Scale). On this scale, 0 dBFS is the absolute maximum volume your system can handle before the audio distorts into an unpleasant, crackling mess (known as "clipping"). Because 0 is the ceiling, all your audio levels will be negative numbers. A typical dialogue track might sit around -12 dBFS to -6 dBFS.

You will also eventually encounter the term **LUFS** (Loudness Units Full Scale). While dBFS measures the exact volume of a single audio peak, LUFS measures the *perceived average loudness* of an entire track. LUFS is how streaming platforms like YouTube and Netflix standardize their audio. For now, we will focus on dBFS for our manual mixing, but keep LUFS in the back of your mind as the ultimate goal for final output.

### Frequency and Hertz (Hz)
Sound travels in waves. **Frequency** refers to how fast those waves vibrate, which determines the pitch of the sound. Frequency is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. A deep bass rumble might be at 60 Hz, a human speaking voice usually sits between 100 Hz and 8,000 Hz (8 kHz), and the sharp hiss of a cymbal can reach 15,000 Hz (15 kHz) or higher. Understanding that audio is made up of different frequencies is the key to using our first major tool: the Equalizer.

## Navigating the Fairlight Page

At the bottom of the DaVinci Resolve interface, click on the **Fairlight Page** icon (it looks like a musical note). When you enter this page, your timeline remains exactly the same as it was on the Edit Page, but the interface transforms into a professional audio mixing environment.

The layout is divided into several key areas:
*   **The Timeline:** Located at the bottom, this is where you will edit audio clips, move them around, and apply keyframes. Notice that audio tracks are much taller here, making it easier to see the audio waveforms.
*   **The Mixer:** Located on the right side. This panel looks like a traditional hardware mixing console, with vertical channel strips for every track in your timeline.
*   **The Inspector and Effects Library:** Located on the top left. Here you can find audio effects, transitions, and track settings.
*   **The Meters:** Located at the top right. These vertical bars show you the real-time volume of your audio, turning green, yellow, and finally red as you approach the 0 dBFS clipping limit.

## Audio Tracks and Patching

If you look at your timeline on the Fairlight Page, you will notice that audio tracks are numbered (A1, A2, A3, etc.). DaVinci Resolve uses a track-based audio architecture. This means that effects and volume adjustments can be applied to individual clips *or* to entire tracks at once.

### Track Types
By default, you are working with standard audio tracks. However, if you right-click on a track header, you will see options to change the track type:
*   **Mono:** Plays out of a single speaker. Perfect for single-person dialogue recorded with one microphone.
*   **Stereo:** Plays out of the left and right speakers. Ideal for background music, sound effects, and ambient room tone.
*   **5.1 or 7.1:** Surround sound tracks, used primarily for film and broadcast. As a beginner, you will mostly use Mono and Stereo tracks.

### The Patching System
When you drag a music file from your Media Pool into the timeline, how does Resolve know whether to put it on a mono track or a stereo track? This is where **patching** comes in.

Patching is the process of routing an audio source to a specific destination track. 

1.  In the timeline track header, look at the **Patch Input/Output** column (it often looks like a small "I/O" or a series of dots). 
2.  If you are bringing in a stereo music track, you want to ensure it is routed to a stereo track. 
3.  You can manually create a new track by right-clicking the track header and selecting "Add Track," choosing Stereo as the format. 
4.  By managing your tracks—keeping all dialogue on mono tracks (A1, A2) and all music on stereo tracks (A3, A4)—you make the mixing process significantly easier. If all your dialogue is on one track, you can apply an effect to that single track to clean up all the dialogue at once, rather than applying it to individual clips.

## The Mixer: Volume and Panning

The **Mixer** is your primary control panel for balancing the different sounds in your video. Each track in your timeline gets its own vertical channel strip in the Mixer.

### Adjusting Clip and Track Volume
There are two main ways to adjust volume in Fairlight: at the clip level and at the track level.

**Clip Volume:** If you just want to make one specific piece of audio louder or quieter, you can adjust the clip volume. In the timeline, grab the thin white horizontal line in the middle of an audio clip and drag it up or down. This is great for quick fixes.

**Track Volume (The Fader):** For broader adjustments, use the **Fader** in the Mixer. The fader is the sliding vertical bar on each channel strip. Sliding it up increases the volume of the entire track; sliding it down decreases it. 

*   **Scenario:** Imagine you have an interviewee on Track 1, and their voice is too quiet. You drag the Track 1 fader up until the meters are peaking around -6 dBFS. However, because you raised the track volume, a faint background hum in their microphone is now also very loud. The Mixer allows you to balance this track against everything else.

### Panning
Next to the fader on the channel strip, you will see the **Pan** control. **Panning** (short for panoramic) dictates where the sound sits in the stereo field—left, right, or center.

*   For **Dialogue**, you generally want the pan set to the dead center (usually a value of 0 or C). Human voices come from a single point, so centering them feels natural.
*   For **Music and Ambience**, you might pan elements slightly to the left or right to create a sense of space. If you have a stereo music track, Resolve automatically handles the left/right distribution, so you leave the pan at center.

## Applying Basic EQ for Voice Clarity

Sometimes, simply turning up the volume isn't enough. If your dialogue sounds muddy, boomy, or harsh, you need to shape the frequencies. This process is called **Equalization (EQ)**. An Equalizer allows you to turn up (boost) or turn down (cut) specific frequencies within an audio track, much like adjusting the bass and treble knobs on a stereo, but with much more precision.

### Accessing the EQ
DaVinci Resolve has a built-in EQ tool readily accessible on the Fairlight Page. Look at the top of your Mixer channel strip. You will see a small "EQ" button. Double-clicking this button opens the **Fairlight EQ** window.

The Fairlight EQ window displays a graph. The horizontal axis represents frequency (from low bass on the left to high treble on the right). The vertical axis represents volume (boost or cut). 

### Enhancing Voice Clarity
When dealing with human speech, certain frequencies cause specific problems. Here is a beginner-friendly workflow for cleaning up dialogue using the EQ bands (the numbered dots on the graph):

1.  **Cut the Low-End Rumble (Band 1):** Air conditioning hums, heavy footsteps, and traffic noise often live below 100 Hz. Human voices don't really need these low frequencies. Click and drag Band 1 (the leftmost dot) down to reduce the low-end rumble. Better yet, change the shape of Band 1 to a "High-Pass Filter" (a curve that slopes down to the right) and set it to around 80 Hz. This completely cuts out everything below 80 Hz while leaving the voice intact.
2.  **Reduce Muddiness (Band 2):** If the voice sounds "muddy" or like the person is speaking in a box, the problem is usually between 200 Hz and 400 Hz. Make a slight cut (dragging the dot down a few decibels) in this range.
3.  **Boost Clarity (Band 3):** The "presence" of the human voice—the part that makes consonants crisp and easy to understand—lives around 2 kHz to 4 kHz. A gentle boost here can make dialogue cut through background music.
4.  **Tame Harshness (Band 4):** If the audio sounds piercing or causes ear fatigue, there might be harsh frequencies around 5 kHz to 8 kHz. Make a small cut here if needed.

**Pro Tip:** When using EQ, it is almost always better to *cut* frequencies you don't want than to *boost* frequencies you do want. Cutting prevents distortion and keeps your audio sounding natural.

## Keyframing: The Audio Ducking Technique

You have balanced your track volumes and cleaned up your dialogue with EQ. But you hit a common snag: the background music is perfectly loud during the intro, but when the speaker starts talking, the music drowns them out. If you turn the music track down, it becomes too quiet during the intro.

The solution is **Audio Ducking**—automatically lowering the music when someone speaks and raising it when they stop. We achieve this using **Keyframes**.

A **keyframe** is a marker you place on the timeline that tells DaVinci Resolve "at this exact moment, make the volume X." By placing two keyframes with different volume values, Resolve will smoothly transition the volume between them.

### How to Keyframe Volume in Fairlight
There are two primary ways to keyframe volume in Fairlight: on the clip itself, or on the track. For beginners, doing it on the clip is the most visual and intuitive method.

1.  **Select the Audio Clip:** Click on the background music clip in your timeline.
2.  **Reveal the Volume Line:** In the timeline track header, ensure the clip volume overlay is visible. You should see a thin horizontal line running through the middle of the audio waveform.
3.  **Add the First Keyframe:** Hold down the **Option** key (Mac) or the **Alt** key (Windows) and click on the volume line where the dialogue is about to begin. A small diamond icon (the keyframe) will appear.
4.  **Add the Second Keyframe:** Move your playhead a second or two later (right as the dialogue starts) and add another keyframe.
5.  **Lower the Volume:** Click and drag the second keyframe downward. You will see the volume line drop. If you play the audio back, the music will now smoothly fade down right as the person speaks.
6.  **Raise it Back Up:** When the dialogue ends, place another keyframe at the lower volume, move a second or two ahead, place a final keyframe, and drag that one back up to the original level.

This manual keyframing process gives you absolute, precise control over your mix. While Fairlight does have an "Audio Ducking" effect in the Effects Library that can automate this process using sidechain compression, learning to do it manually with keyframes is an essential skill that translates to mixing music, sound effects, and complex scenes.

### Track Automation
If you have a very long project with lots of dialogue, keyframing every single clip can be tedious. An alternative is to use **Track Automation**. 

In the Mixer, at the bottom of each channel strip, you will see a dropdown menu that likely says "Off". If you change this to "Volume", Resolve will record the movements of the fader in real-time. You can play the timeline, physically move the fader up and down with your mouse as the video plays, and Resolve will write those movements as keyframes onto the track itself. If you make a mistake, simply switch the dropdown back to "Off", move the fader, and try again.

## Putting It All Together: A Mixing Workflow

When you are ready to finalize your audio, it helps to follow a consistent order of operations. A standard beginner mixing workflow looks like this:

1.  **Organize:** Ensure all dialogue is on specific tracks (e.g., A1 and A2) and all music/sound effects are on separate tracks (e.g., A3 and A4). 
2.  **Clean:** Go to your dialogue tracks and open the Fairlight EQ. Apply a high-pass filter to remove low-end rumble, and make minor cuts for muddiness or harshness. Do this on the *track* level so it applies to all dialogue clips.
3.  **Set Levels:** Play your timeline from the beginning. Look at the meters. Set your dialogue track fader so the loudest parts peak around -6 dBFS. Next, bring up your music track fader until the music sounds balanced, but ensure it doesn't push your master meter into the red (clipping).
4.  **Duck the Music:** Use keyframes to lower the music track whenever dialogue is present.
5.  **Final Check:** Close your eyes and just listen. Does anything distract you? Is the dialogue crystal clear? Make minor fader adjustments until the mix sounds cohesive.

Audio mixing is as much an art as it is a technical process. Your ears are your most important tool. If it sounds good, it is good. Don't be afraid to experiment with the faders and EQ settings to hear how they change the soundscape.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Fairlight Page** is DaVinci Resolve’s dedicated audio environment, equipped with professional tools for mixing, EQ, and sound design, while keeping your timeline intact.
*   **dBFS (Decibels Full Scale)** measures digital audio volume, where 0 dBFS is the maximum limit before clipping. Keep your dialogue peaking around -6 dBFS to -12 dBFS.
*   **Tracks and Patching:** Organizing audio by type (e.g., dialogue on mono tracks, music on stereo tracks) simplifies mixing and allows you to apply effects to entire tracks at once.
*   **The Mixer** uses faders to control track volume and pan controls to place sound in the left, right, or center of the stereo field. Dialogue should almost always be panned to the center.
*   **Equalization (EQ)** allows you to cut or boost specific frequencies. Cutting low-frequency rumble and slightly boosting presence frequencies (2-4 kHz) is the best way to enhance voice clarity.
*   **Keyframing** is the process of placing markers on the timeline to automate volume changes, allowing you to smoothly "duck" background music under dialogue.

# Module 7: Introduction to Color Correction

Imagine this: You’ve spent weeks planning a short film. You wake up before dawn to catch the perfect morning light, your actors deliver their lines flawlessly, and your camera operator nails the framing. You sit down at your computer, import your footage, and suddenly your heart sinks. The indoor scenes look orange. The outdoor scenes look slightly blue and washed out. The morning sunlight you chased so eagerly looks flat and gray on the screen. 

This is the reality of raw video. Cameras, no matter how advanced, do not see the world the way human eyes do. Our brains automatically adjust to make a piece of paper look white whether we are under a yellow tungsten lightbulb or a blue overcast sky. Cameras simply record the light bouncing off objects, which means different light sources cast different color temperatures onto your footage.

The good news is that this is exactly what the **Color Page** in DaVinci Resolve was built to fix. In fact, color correction is so central to this software that the entire application is named after the DaVinci color grading systems that revolutionized the film industry. Now that you have assembled your timeline, added your titles, and mixed your audio, it is time to breathe visual life into your project.

## The Color Page Interface

When you click the **Color Page** icon at the bottom of the screen—represented by three colored circles—your workspace transforms. While the Edit Page was designed for cutting and arranging, the Color Page is a specialized environment for manipulating the color and brightness of your clips.

The interface is divided into several key areas:

*   **The Timeline:** Located at the bottom, this mirrors the timeline from your Edit Page. Whatever clips you have cut and arranged will appear here. Clicking a clip in this timeline makes it active for color correction.
*   **The Viewer:** Situated in the top right, this is where you see your video. It updates in real-time as you make adjustments.
*   **The Scopes:** Located in the top left, these are scientific graphs that show you exactly what is happening in your video's color and brightness. 
*   **The Nodes:** Situated in the middle, this is the heart of DaVinci Resolve’s color workflow. It is a visual flowchart where you build your corrections.
*   **The Color Wheels:** Located in the bottom left, these are your primary tools for adjusting the image. 

## Understanding the Node-Based Workflow

If you have ever used photo editing software like Adobe Photoshop, you are likely familiar with layers, where corrections are stacked on top of one another. DaVinci Resolve does not use layers. Instead, it uses a **node-based workflow**.

A **node** is essentially a container for a single adjustment. Think of a node as a transparent sheet of glass. On one sheet, you might fix the brightness. On another sheet, you might fix the white balance. On a third, you might add a creative filter. 

These sheets are connected in a sequence by wires. The video signal flows from the left side of the screen, enters the first node (where your first adjustment is applied), flows out of that node into the next node, and continues until it reaches the Viewer. 

### Why Nodes?

Nodes are incredibly powerful because they are non-destructive and highly organized. If you decide you don't like the contrast adjustment you made, you don't have to undo a dozen other changes to get rid of it. You simply bypass or delete the specific node containing that contrast adjustment. 

Every clip you select will start with one default node, labeled **Node 1**. To add a new node, you can use the keyboard shortcut **Alt+S** (Option+S on Mac). A new node will appear to the right of your first node, automatically wired up and ready for your next adjustment. 

## Reading the Scopes: The Truth-Tellers

When it comes to color, your eyes can easily lie to you. If you stare at a bright red wall for thirty seconds and then look at a white wall, the white wall will temporarily look cyan. Similarly, if you are editing in a dark room, your eyes will adjust, making a dimly lit video look brighter than it actually is.

To fix this, we rely on **scopes**. Scopes are objective, mathematical representations of your video's luminance (brightness) and chrominance (color). They do not get tired, and they are not influenced by the lighting in your room.

### The Waveform Monitor

The most important scope for evaluating exposure is the **Waveform Monitor**. It displays the brightness of your image from left to right. 

*   **The horizontal axis** represents the left-to-right position of pixels in your video frame. If a bright window is on the right side of your video, you will see a bright spike on the right side of the Waveform.
*   **The vertical axis** represents brightness, measured in IRE (a standard video measurement). The bottom of the scale (0 IRE) is pure black. The top of the scale (100 IRE) is pure white.
*   **The middle** represents mid-tones, like skin tones and average lighting.

By looking at the Waveform, you can tell exactly how dark your shadows are and how bright your highlights are, without guessing. If your video looks dark on your monitor, but the Waveform shows the image reaching all the way to the top, you know your physical monitor is the problem, not the video.

### The Vectorscope

While the Waveform tells you about brightness, the **Vectorscope** tells you about color. It is a circular graph that maps out the colors in your image. 

*   The center of the circle represents zero color (pure black, white, or gray).
*   As colors become more saturated, they push outward from the center toward the edges.
*   The circle has targets marked with letters like R, G, B (Red, Green, Blue) and a line marked **T**. The T line is arguably the most important part of this scope: it represents the exact hue of human skin tones. No matter a person's ethnicity, a correctly balanced video will show their skin tone resting right on this T line.

## Step 1: Correcting Exposure and Contrast

The first step in any color workflow is fixing the light. There is no point in fixing the color of a shot if the shot is too dark to see it. We do this using the **Color Wheels**.

The Color Wheels section is divided into four main wheels: **Lift**, **Gamma**, **Gain**, and **Offset**. 

*   **Lift** controls the shadows (the darkest parts of your image).
*   **Gamma** controls the midtones (the middle range, where most of your image lives).
*   **Gain** controls the highlights (the brightest parts of your image).
*   **Offset** adjusts the brightness of the entire image equally.

Beneath each wheel is a **Master Wheel**, a horizontal slider that adjusts the brightness of that specific tonal range. Pushing the slider to the left darkens that range; pushing it to the right brightens it.

### A Concrete Scenario: Fixing an Underexposed Shot

Imagine you have a clip of an interview, but the subject's face is hidden in shadow. You look at your Waveform Monitor and notice that the highest points of the image only reach about 60 IRE, and the shadows are crushed flat at the very bottom. 

1.  Select the clip in your timeline.
2.  On **Node 1**, drag the **Lift Master Wheel** slightly to the right. This lifts the shadows, revealing detail in the subject's face. On the Waveform, you will see the bottom of the graph rise slightly.
3.  Drag the **Gain Master Wheel** to the right to brighten the highlights. You want the brightest part of the image—perhaps a window in the background or a bright shirt—to approach 90 to 100 IRE.
4.  Use the **Gamma Master Wheel** to adjust the overall midtones until the subject's face looks properly exposed.

### Adding Contrast

Often, raw video looks flat and gray. To fix this, we need to increase the **contrast**—the difference between the darkest and lightest parts of the image. 

Below the Color Wheels, you will find the **Contrast** slider. 
*   Dragging the Contrast slider to the right makes the darks darker and the lights lighter, adding punch and depth to the image. 
*   Alternatively, you can pull the **Lift Master Wheel** down slightly and push the **Gain Master Wheel** up slightly to manually create a larger gap between your shadows and highlights on the Waveform.

## Step 2: Fixing White Balance

Once your exposure is balanced, it is time to fix the color cast. **White balance** refers to the process of telling the software what true white is in your scene. Once the software knows what white looks like, all other colors will fall into place.

If you shot indoors without adjusting your camera's white balance, your footage might look overly orange (due to tungsten lightbulbs). If you shot in the shade outdoors, it might look overly blue.

### Temperature and Tint Controls

In DaVinci Resolve, white balance is primarily adjusted using two sliders found below the Color Wheels: **Temperature** and **Tint**.

*   **Temperature** shifts the image along the blue-to-yellow axis. Dragging it to the left adds blue (cooling the image). Dragging it to the right adds yellow/orange (warming the image).
*   **Tint** shifts the image along the green-to-magenta axis. Dragging it to the left adds green. Dragging it to the right adds magenta.

### The Manual Approach

Let’s say you have an indoor shot that looks incredibly orange. 
1. Create a new node (**Alt+S** / **Option+S**) so your white balance correction is separate from your exposure fix. 
2. Grab the **Temperature** slider and drag it to the left. As you add blue, the overwhelming orange cast will begin to neutralize. 
3. Look at the Vectorscope. You want the graph to pull closer to the center, reducing the heavy saturation of the orange/yellow colors. 
4. If the image still has a slight green tint (common under fluorescent office lights), drag the **Tint** slider slightly to the right to introduce magenta, neutralizing the green.

### The Automatic Approach: The Color Picker

Sometimes, guessing with sliders is tedious. DaVinci Resolve has a powerful automatic white balance tool. 

1. Create a new node for this adjustment.
2. In the top left corner of the Color Wheels panel, look for the **Color Picker** icon (it looks like an eyedropper).
3. Click the eyedropper, then click on a part of your video in the Viewer that is supposed to be pure white or neutral gray. A white wall, a gray t-shirt, or a piece of paper works perfectly. 

Resolve will automatically analyze that pixel and adjust the Temperature and Tint sliders to force that pixel to true neutral. This is often the fastest and most accurate way to fix a bad color cast.

## Step 3: Applying a Basic LUT for Creative Styling

Up to this point, we have been doing **color correction**—the technical process of fixing exposure and white balance so the image looks natural and accurate. Now, we move into the realm of **color grading**—the creative process of adding a stylistic look to your video.

Think of color correction as washing a canvas so it is clean. Color grading is painting your artistic vision onto that clean canvas. 

One of the most common and efficient ways to apply a creative style is by using a **LUT**, which stands for **Look Up Table**. 

### What is a LUT?

A LUT is essentially a preset file. It is a mathematical formula that takes the original colors of your video and replaces them with new colors. For example, a LUT might say, "Every time you see a dark gray pixel, make it slightly blue. Every time you see a bright pink pixel, make it orange."

Filmmakers use LUTs to quickly apply cinematic looks, such as the teal-and-orange look popular in action movies, or the high-contrast, desaturated look common in dramas.

### How to Apply a LUT

DaVinci Resolve comes with dozens of built-in LUTs, and you can easily add your own. 

1. Create a new node at the end of your node tree. This is crucial: **LUTs should generally be applied after you have corrected your exposure and white balance.** If you apply a LUT to uncorrected footage, the LUT will amplify your mistakes.
2. In the top left of the Color Page, locate the **LUT Browser**. 
3. Click to open it, and you will see folders containing various LUT categories, such as "Emulate Film" or "DaVinci Color Space Transforms."
4. Click on a LUT to see a preview of how it will affect your image.
5. To apply the LUT, simply drag and drop it from the browser directly onto your new node. 

When you drop the LUT onto the node, the node will update to show the LUT's name. You will immediately see the creative style applied to your footage in the Viewer.

### Adjusting the LUT's Intensity

Sometimes a LUT is too strong. It might make the colors too saturated or the contrast too high. Because you applied the LUT to its own dedicated node, you have complete control over it.

If you look at the node, you will see a small label showing the LUT's name. Next to it is a **Key** button (which looks like a small gray triangle). If you click this, a small panel opens allowing you to adjust the **Key Output Gain**. Lowering this slider reduces the opacity of the LUT, blending it with your original corrected footage so you can find the perfect balance.

## Putting It All Together: The Node Flow

Let’s review the logical flow of the node tree you have just built. If you look at the middle of your Color Page, you should see a clean, sequential line of nodes:

1.  **Node 1 (Exposure & Contrast):** The video signal enters here. You used the Lift, Gamma, and Gain master wheels to fix the brightness, and the Contrast slider to add depth.
2.  **Node 2 (White Balance):** The corrected signal flows into this node. You used the Temperature and Tint sliders (or the color picker) to remove unwanted color casts and make whites look truly white.
3.  **Node 3 (LUT/Styling):** The balanced signal flows into this final node. You applied a LUT to give the footage a cinematic, creative style.

This sequential, container-based approach is the foundation of all professional color grading in DaVinci Resolve. If a director asks you to make the overall image slightly brighter, you go back to Node 1. If they want the look to be less aggressive, you adjust Node 3. Every change is isolated, organized, and entirely non-destructive.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Nodes vs. Layers:** DaVinci Resolve uses a node-based workflow, where adjustments are made in individual, connected containers rather than stacked layers. Use **Alt+S** (Option+S) to add new nodes.
*   **Trust the Scopes:** Your eyes can deceive you. Use the **Waveform Monitor** to objectively measure and fix exposure (brightness), and use the **Vectorscope** to measure color and skin tones.
*   **Color Wheels:** Use **Lift** for shadows, **Gamma** for midtones, and **Gain** for highlights. The Master Wheels beneath them adjust brightness, while the wheels themselves adjust color.
*   **White Balance:** Fix color casts using the **Temperature** (blue/yellow) and **Tint** (green/magenta) sliders, or use the **Color Picker** tool to automatically neutralize a white or gray pixel.
*   **Correction vs. Grading:** Color correction is the technical balancing of light and color. Color grading is the creative styling of the image.
*   **LUTs:** A **Look Up Table** is a preset that instantly applies a creative color style. Always apply LUTs on a new node *after* you have corrected your exposure and white balance.
